metric_name: U.S. gas and electric utility revenues
metric_value: $300 billion+
notes: Cited as last major U.S. monopoly to be deregulated (after banking telecom transportation)

metric_value: Utilities going same route as banking telecommunications and transportation
notes: Signals structural inevitability of competitive markets

metric_value: Conservative; many will wait until forced by deregulation; some will then outsource
notes: Coexists with urgent near-term spending on billing CIS and call centers

metric_value: Essential for marketers and distributors; regulated utilities with archaic systems must aggressively compete
notes: First time utilities competing for customers across broad base; outcome unverified

metric_value: Provide new distribution mechanism feeding back-end billing and operational support; strategic IT asset
notes: In wide use among large customers; foundation for competitive retail electricity distribution

metric_value: Utilities will gradually integrate existing specialized apps over networks using new infrastructures and ERP
notes: Asset management and accounting cited as early consolidation areas; outcome unverified

metric_value: Mature; new versions of controls systems will link to heterogeneous environments for ERP purposes
notes: Operational control and distribution management systems will evolve rapidly

metric_value: Will ultimately become essential; moving from paper to e-commerce with real-time transactions
notes: Links electronic contracting to operational delivery mechanisms; outcome unverified

metric_value: Real-time operational systems (EMS) raise most dire Y2K possibilities; driving system replacement
notes: Embedded real-time systems difficult to assess; embedded controls for generation and T&D at risk

metric_value: Cost control needs from deregulation plus customizable app suites incentivize shift from proprietary
notes: Impeded by large existing capital investments; tradition of slow IT change and custom development

metric_value: Make sense because utilities have facilities and expertise giving head start in telecom business
notes: Dual purpose: internal system-wide controls + external telecom revenue opportunity

metric_value: Strong global presence; unique position to support large facilities engineering contracts with IT
notes: 1997 ABB revenues $33.7B total; Alpha meter data management system under development

metric_value: Well-positioned to sell/integrate software; strategic consultant for restructuring decisions
notes: CUSTOMER/1 billing/CIS used by utilities throughout U.S.; outsourced billing under development

metric_name: IBM Utility and Energy Services scale
metric_value: 1500+ people worldwide; ~$2 billion revenues; large component of utility data center outsourcing
notes: Shifted from hardware to consulting integration and managed services; lead integrator for Western Exchange

metric_value: 350+ utility customers worldwide; SAP Utilities 1998 release features purchasing accounting asset management HR
notes: RIVA European package basis; numerous U.S. utilities adopting SAP R/3; Aberdeen views initial emphasis on work management and customer care as well-matched

metric_value: Growing very rapidly; best for AS/400 users; well-suited for mid-sized T&D companies
notes: Modules for utility CIS billing metered data market segmentation and work order processing

metric_value: Increasingly popular; playing important role in integration and data conversion projects involving multiple suppliers
notes: Object-based GIS linking RDBMS to spatial data; eliminates redundancy; UNIX and NT

metric_name: Supplier success factors for utility IT
metric_value: Must leverage existing industry competencies; offer integration/outsourcing; provide pre-packaged object-based solutions
notes: Specialized channels required; solutions must mirror economic priorities of changing industry

metric_value: Utilities selling generation assets; ISOs overseeing regional power markets; next-hour trading
notes: Wall Street driving electricity to commodity status as done with natural gas

metric_value: Partnered to market transmission trading service for utilities; IBM is lead integrator for Western Exchange
notes: Illustrates convergence of IT and energy transaction systems
